nodes.name: Offshore Survey USV-ROV
nodes.description:
The system is a hybrid diesel-electric Unmanned Surface Vessel (USV) and tethered light work-class Remotely Operated Vehicle (ROV), operated in concert from a shore-based Remote Operations Centre (ROC) for offshore benthic habitat and environmental survey missions at ranges of 50–200 km from shore. The USV serves as the primary sea-surface platform, providing propulsion, power generation, a stern-ramp Launch and Recovery System (LARS) for the ROV, and a communications relay node between the ROV and the ROC. The ROC provides all mission planning, supervisory control, and payload data reception. The USV operates under supervised autonomy — executing operator-defined waypoints while autonomously managing station-keeping, collision avoidance, and fault responses — with the primary long-range link to the ROC provided by a LEO satellite constellation (e.g. Starlink or Iridium) at approximately 40–80 ms latency. The system is subject to full classification society oversight [assumed: DNV or Lloyd's Register unmanned vessel notation], which governs the rigour of the collision avoidance system, safety case, and design documentation programme.

The ROV is a light work-class vehicle (50–200 kg) deployed via the stern ramp on a fibre-optic management tether [assumed: tether carries high-voltage power and bidirectional data], capable of operating to depths of 300–1000 m. Its primary mission payload is seabed habitat and benthic survey, comprising high-definition video cameras, multibeam or imaging sonar, and sediment sampling equipment [specific payload configuration to be confirmed]. Onboard edge processing on the USV compresses and prioritises video and sonar data before uplink to the ROC, managing the bandwidth constraints of the satellite link. The USV is designed to transit in sea states up to Sea State 6 (Hs ~6 m), with ROV launch and recovery via the stern ramp restricted to Sea State 4 (Hs ~2.5 m) or below. In the event of a communications loss, both vehicles enter a hold state — the USV maintains station and the ROV holds depth — awaiting link restoration up to a defined timeout, after which both vehicles execute an autonomous return-to-surface and home sequence.

The USV power architecture is hybrid diesel-electric with significant battery capacity, sized to supply both vessel hotel loads and ROV tether power simultaneously [assumed: 5–20 kW delivered to ROV at operating depth via high-voltage DC tether]. Three architectural parameters remain open and will shape detailed design: (1) mission endurance per deployment, which drives fuel tankage, battery sizing, and autonomous energy management requirements; (2) the classification society scope — whether it covers the USV only or extends to the LARS, ROV, and ROC cybersecurity architecture; and (3) the operational logistics model — whether the USV operates from a fixed shore base, a support vessel, or a hybrid of both — which determines transit endurance requirements and the degree of autonomous self-sufficiency required between deployments.

sections.title: Purpose & Scope
sections.content: The Offshore Survey USV-ROV is a hybrid diesel-electric unmanned surface vessel paired with a tethered light work-class ROV, designed to conduct benthic habitat and environmental survey missions at 50–200 km offshore without personnel at sea. The USV acts as the sea-surface host platform and communications relay, while the ROV performs subsea survey to depths of 300–1000 m. All mission planning, supervisory control, and payload data reception are handled from a shore-based Remote Operations Centre (ROC). The system is subject to classification society oversight (DNV or Lloyd's Register unmanned vessel notation), establishing the regulatory boundary for the safety case and design documentation programme.

sections.title: Capabilities
sections.content: The USV transits at up to 10 knots in sea states up to SS6 (Hs ~6 m) and maintains station during ROV operations under supervised autonomy, executing operator-defined waypoints with autonomous collision avoidance at a minimum detection range of 2,000 m. The ROV (estimated 120 kg in air) operates to 1,000 m depth on a 1,200 m fibre-optic management tether, receiving 5–20 kW of HVDC power and carrying HD cameras, multibeam/imaging sonar, and sediment sampling equipment. Onboard edge processing compresses approximately 500 Mbps of raw sensor data to fit within the ~50 Mbps satellite uplink budget before transmission to the ROC. On communications loss, both vehicles autonomously hold position and execute a return-to-home sequence after a defined timeout.

sections.title: Architecture Summary
sections.content: The system is decomposed into six subsystems. The USV Platform (SUB-USV) provides the hull, propulsion, and station-keeping foundation. Power Generation & Distribution (SUB-PWR) supplies a hybrid diesel-electric architecture with an estimated 60 kW generation capacity and 80 kWh battery bank, feeding both vessel hotel loads (~15 kW) and the ROV tether (~12 kW nominal). The ROV Vehicle (SUB-ROV) is the subsea survey element. The Launch & Recovery System (SUB-LARS) provides the stern-ramp mechanism rated to SS4 and capable of handling ROVs up to 250 kg. Communications & Data Link (SUB-COMMS) manages the LEO satellite primary link and backup 4G/LTE or VSAT links. Autonomy, Control & Mission Payload (SUB-AUTO) integrates navigation, collision avoidance, fault management, and edge data processing across all subsystems.

sections.title: Interface Summary
sections.content: Two primary system-level interfaces have been defined. IF-001 (ROV Tether Power & Data) carries HVDC power from SUB-PWR to the ROV and bidirectional fibre-optic data including HD video, sonar streams, 100 Hz control commands, and 10 Hz ROV telemetry. IF-002 (Processed Payload & Command Relay) is an Ethernet/IP link between SUB-AUTO and SUB-COMMS, carrying compressed video and sonar data for satellite uplink alongside inbound ROC operator commands and combined USV/ROV telemetry at 1 Hz. Additional internal interfaces between SUB-AUTO and propulsion, LARS sequencing, and navigation sensors remain to be formally defined.

sections.title: Constraints & Limitations
sections.content: ROV launch and recovery is restricted to SS4 (Hs ≤ 2.5 m); operations in higher sea states require the ROV to hold depth or abort to surface. The LEO satellite link imposes a nominal 40–80 ms one-way latency and an uplink cap of approximately 50 Mbps, requiring onboard compression to be effective before any real-time ROV piloting is feasible. Classification society scope (DNV/LR unmanned notation) constrains the collision avoidance system design, cybersecurity architecture, and the extent of the safety case — the precise scope covering LARS, ROV, and ROC has not yet been confirmed. The system must comply with COLREGS for autonomous surface navigation, which places minimum performance requirements on the collision avoidance detection range and response time.

sections.title: Open Issues & Risks
sections.content: Three architectural parameters remain unresolved and represent the highest-priority design decisions. First, mission endurance per deployment has not been defined, leaving fuel tankage, battery sizing, and autonomous energy management requirements unconstrained. Second, the classification society scope is unconfirmed — whether it extends beyond the USV hull to cover the LARS, ROV, and ROC cybersecurity architecture will materially affect the design documentation programme and cost. Third, the operational logistics model (fixed shore base vs. support vessel vs. hybrid) determines transit endurance requirements and the degree of autonomous self-sufficiency needed between deployments. Additionally, the specific ROV payload configuration (camera types, sonar model, sediment sampler) has not been confirmed, which affects tether power sizing and edge processing compression ratios. Internal interfaces between SUB-AUTO and propulsion/LARS/navigation sensors have not yet been formally defined.

_narrativeAnalysis.mainstaySentence: The Power Generation & Distribution energises and commands the ROV Vehicle, whose video and sonar returns the Autonomy, Control & Mission Payload edge-processes and compresses, which the Communications & Data Link relays as processed survey data to the remote operations centre ashore.

cards.title: The Survey Chain: Power to Insight
cards.body: The central purpose of the Offshore Survey USV-ROV is to collect high-fidelity subsea environmental data and deliver it, in near-real time, to shore-based operators. That purpose is served by a two-link mainstay. First, Power Generation & Distribution drives the tether, pushing HVDC power down to the ROV Vehicle while simultaneously carrying control commands and receiving raw video and sonar data back up the same fibre-optic core. Second, Autonomy, Control & Mission Payload ingests those raw streams, applies onboard edge processing, and hands compressed, mission-ready data to Communications & Data Link for transmission to the remote operations centre. Every other subsystem in the architecture exists to keep this chain intact and productive.

cards.title: The Tether Interface: Power and Perception in One Bundle
cards.body: Interface IF-001 is the physical and logical spine of the system. Power Generation & Distribution does not merely supply electricity to the ROV Vehicle — it also acts as the conduit for bidirectional mission data. HVDC power flows down the tether to sustain the ROV's thrusters, lights, and sensors, while rov_control_commands issued by the autonomy layer travel the same path to steer the vehicle. In return, rov_video_sonar_data and rov_telemetry ascend the fibre-optic core, making the tether simultaneously a power umbilical and a high-bandwidth sensor highway. This tight coupling means that any interruption to Power Generation & Distribution is an immediate threat not just to propulsion but to the entire survey data pipeline.

cards.title: Autonomy and Communications: Processing Before Transmission
cards.body: Interface IF-002 marks the boundary between raw data and actionable intelligence. Autonomy, Control & Mission Payload performs onboard edge processing — compressing video, filtering sonar returns, and fusing USV and ROV telemetry — before handing the resulting streams to Communications & Data Link. This architecture deliberately reduces the bandwidth burden on the long-range link: compressed_video_stream and compressed_sonar_data are transmission-ready products, not raw sensor dumps. Equally important, the interface is bidirectional: roc_operator_commands flow inbound from the shore-based remote operations centre through Communications & Data Link to the autonomy layer, allowing supervised override of autonomous behaviours without requiring a direct operator-to-ROV link.

cards.title: Narrative Islands: USV Platform and LARS
cards.body: Two subsystems — USV Platform and Launch & Recovery System — currently appear as narrative islands: they carry no modelled interfaces in this graph. This does not mean they are unimportant; on the contrary, USV Platform provides the hull, propulsion, and station-keeping that physically positions the entire system over the survey site, and Launch & Recovery System enables the safe deployment and retrieval of the ROV Vehicle in sea states up to SS4. Their absence from the interface graph indicates a modelling gap rather than a functional one. Future iterations should capture, at minimum, the power draw of USV Platform from Power Generation & Distribution, the mechanical and control signals between LARS and Autonomy, Control & Mission Payload, and the navigation data exchange between USV Platform and the autonomy layer.

nodes.name: USV Platform
nodes.description: Provides the sea-surface vessel hull, propulsion, and station-keeping capability for the system. The USV is a hybrid diesel-electric vessel designed to transit in sea states up to SS6 and maintain station during ROV operations. It interfaces with the Power Generation, ROV, LARS, and Communications subsystems as the primary physical host platform.

nodes.name: Power Generation & Distribution
nodes.description: Generates, stores, and distributes electrical power for all USV and ROV loads via a hybrid diesel-electric architecture. The subsystem comprises diesel generators, a significant battery bank, and a high-voltage DC distribution bus that supplies both vessel hotel loads and the ROV tether power feed (5–20 kW). It interfaces with the USV Platform for mechanical mounting, the ROV via the tether power conductor, and the Autonomy & Control subsystem for energy management commands.

nodes.name: ROV Vehicle
nodes.description: Provides the subsea vehicle capable of operating to 300–1000 m depth for benthic habitat and environmental survey missions. The ROV is a light work-class vehicle (50–200 kg) equipped with HD cameras, multibeam/imaging sonar, and sediment sampling equipment, receiving power and exchanging data via a fibre-optic management tether. It interfaces with the LARS subsystem for launch and recovery, the Power Generation subsystem via the tether HVDC feed, and the Mission Payload & Data subsystem for sensor data upward and control commands downward.

sections.title: Purpose & Scope
sections.content: The ROV Vehicle subsystem is the subsea survey element of the Offshore Survey USV-ROV system, providing a tethered, remotely operated platform capable of conducting benthic habitat and environmental surveys at depths of 300–1000 m. It exists to extend the system's operational reach below the sea surface, carrying scientific payload to the seabed while the USV remains at the surface as host and relay. The ROV operates under continuous supervisory control from the shore-based ROC, with all power and bidirectional data exchanged via a fibre-optic management tether deployed and recovered by the LARS subsystem. Its scope covers the vehicle structure, thrusters, pressure housings, onboard electronics, and integrated survey payload.

sections.title: Capabilities
sections.content: The ROV is a light work-class vehicle with an estimated in-air mass of 120 kg (within the 50–200 kg class envelope) and a pressure rating of 1000 m (100 bar), satisfying REQ-002. It receives 12 kW nominal HVDC power via the management tether (range 5–20 kW) and consumes approximately 10 kW at survey speed across hotel loads and thrusters. The survey payload suite comprises HD video cameras, multibeam or imaging sonar, and sediment sampling equipment, generating an estimated 500 Mbps of raw data that is forwarded to the USV edge processor for compression before satellite uplink. The vehicle supports 100 Hz control command ingestion and 10 Hz telemetry output over the fibre-optic data channel.

sections.title: Architecture Summary
sections.content: The ROV Vehicle subsystem has not yet been formally decomposed into child assemblies; the current model treats it as a single subsystem node within the system hierarchy. Functionally, the vehicle integrates a thruster array for six-degree-of-freedom manoeuvring, a pressure-rated electronics housing for control and payload electronics, a fibre-optic tether termination and power conversion unit, and the survey payload bay. The tether connects the ROV to the LARS tether management system (SUB-LARS) topside, which manages deployment, recovery, and tether storage. Decomposition into assemblies — such as vehicle frame, propulsion, electronics housing, and payload bay — is a recommended next step as the design matures.

sections.title: Interface Summary
sections.content: The ROV Vehicle has one formally defined system-level interface: IF-001 (ROV Tether Power & Data), a combined HVDC power and fibre-optic data link sourced from SUB-PWR. This interface carries continuous HVDC power to the ROV, continuous HD video and sonar data streams from the ROV to the USV, 100 Hz control commands from the USV to the ROV, and 10 Hz ROV telemetry in return. A physical handling interface with SUB-LARS exists during launch and recovery operations — the LARS stern ramp, winch, and tether management system engage the ROV directly — but this interface has not yet been formally defined as an IF entity. No direct electrical or data interface between the ROV and SUB-COMMS or SUB-AUTO is defined; all data routing is mediated by SUB-PWR and SUB-AUTO topside.

sections.title: Constraints & Limitations
sections.content: The ROV is depth-rated to 1000 m; all pressure housings and penetrators must be tested to 100 bar in accordance with the applicable classification society standard (REQ-002). Vehicle mass must remain within the LARS maximum handling capacity of 250 kg in air (REQ-003, REQ-007), providing a 130 kg margin above the current 120 kg estimate for payload growth. Launch and recovery are restricted to SS4 (Hs ≤ 2.5 m) by the LARS operational limit; in higher sea states the ROV must hold depth or abort to surface. The tether length is constrained to 1200 m maximum by the LARS tether management system (REQ-009), which sets the practical operational depth limit when accounting for current-induced catenary offset. The specific payload configuration — camera types, sonar model, and sediment sampler — has not been confirmed, leaving tether power sizing and raw data rate estimates subject to revision.

sections.title: Verification Status
sections.content: Two system-level requirements directly govern the ROV Vehicle: REQ-002 (ROV Operating Depth, 1000 m) and the tether length constraint embedded in REQ-009. Neither has an associated verification record defined at this stage; both are expected to be verified by test during acceptance trials — REQ-002 via a functional dive to 1000 m with all payload systems operational, and REQ-009 via OTDR fibre attenuation measurement after a 50-cycle tether endurance test on the LARS rig. No VER- entities have been created for SUB-ROV requirements, representing a gap in the verification coverage that should be addressed as the design progresses.

sections.title: Open Issues & Risks
sections.content: The ROV payload configuration (camera specification, sonar model, sediment sampler type) remains unconfirmed, which directly affects tether power budget, raw data rate, and the compression ratio required from the SUB-AUTO edge processor — this is the highest-priority open item for the ROV subsystem. The physical LARS-to-ROV handling interface has not been formally defined as an interface entity, leaving the mechanical and structural coupling between the two subsystems undocumented. The ROV subsystem has not been decomposed into child assemblies, meaning no mass, power, or volume budgets have been allocated at the assembly level. Additionally, the classification society scope — whether it extends to the ROV pressure housings and electronics — has not been confirmed, which may impose additional design and documentation requirements on the vehicle.

nodes.name: Launch & Recovery System
nodes.description: Provides the stern-ramp mechanism for safe deployment and recovery of the ROV in sea states up to SS4 (Hs ~2.5 m). The LARS includes the stern ramp structure, winch, tether management system (TMS), and motion compensation or A-frame as required to protect the ROV during water entry and exit. It interfaces with the USV Platform structurally, the ROV Vehicle physically during handling, and the Autonomy & Control subsystem for launch/recovery sequencing commands.

nodes.name: Communications & Data Link
nodes.description: Provides all long-range and local communications links, acting as the relay node between the ROV, USV, and the shore-based ROC. The primary long-range link uses a LEO satellite constellation (Starlink/Iridium) at 40–80 ms latency; secondary/backup links (e.g. 4G/LTE or VSAT) provide redundancy. It interfaces with the Mission Payload & Data subsystem to receive compressed video and sonar data for uplink, and with the Autonomy & Control subsystem to relay operator commands and telemetry.

nodes.name: Autonomy, Control & Mission Payload
nodes.description: Provides supervised autonomy for USV navigation, station-keeping, collision avoidance, fault management, and onboard edge processing of survey payload data. This subsystem executes operator-defined waypoints, manages autonomous hold and return-to-home sequences on comms loss, and compresses/prioritises video and sonar data to fit within satellite link bandwidth constraints. It interfaces with all other subsystems: commanding propulsion and LARS, receiving sensor and payload data from the ROV, and exchanging telemetry and mission data with the ROC via the Communications subsystem.

requirements.title: USV Transit Speed
requirements.description: The Offshore Survey USV-ROV shall achieve a sustained transit speed of not less than 10 knots in sea states up to and including Sea State 4 (significant wave height Hs ≤ 2.5 m).
requirements.rationale: A minimum transit speed of 10 knots is required to reach survey sites 50–200 km offshore within an operationally acceptable transit window and to maintain schedule across a range of typical North Sea or equivalent sea conditions.
acceptanceCriteria: The USV sustains ≥ 10 knots over a 30-minute measured run in SS4 conditions (Hs ≤ 2.5 m) during sea trials.
Speed is measured as GPS-derived speed over ground averaged over the trial run, with no more than ±0.5 knot variation.

requirements.title: ROV Operating Depth
requirements.description: The Offshore Survey USV-ROV shall deploy the ROV to a maximum operating depth of 1000 m below mean sea level.
requirements.rationale: The benthic habitat survey mission requires access to continental shelf and upper slope environments; a 1000 m depth rating covers the full intended survey envelope and aligns with the light work-class ROV specification.
acceptanceCriteria: The ROV pressure housing and all penetrators are rated and tested to 1000 m (100 bar) in accordance with the applicable classification society pressure test standard.
The ROV successfully completes a functional dive to 1000 m depth during acceptance trials, with all payload systems operational at depth.

requirements.title: LARS Sea State Operational Limit
requirements.description: The Offshore Survey USV-ROV shall complete ROV launch and recovery operations via the stern-ramp LARS in sea states up to and including Sea State 4 (significant wave height Hs ≤ 2.5 m).
requirements.rationale: Safe ROV handling at the sea surface is the primary operational constraint on mission availability; SS4 is the accepted industry limit for light work-class ROV LARS operations and is required to achieve acceptable mission uptime in the target operating area.
acceptanceCriteria: The LARS successfully completes a minimum of 3 consecutive launch-and-recovery cycles with the ROV at its maximum handling mass (250 kg) in simulated or measured SS4 conditions (Hs ≤ 2.5 m) without damage to the ROV, tether, or LARS structure.
Peak dynamic load on the LARS winch during SS4 trials does not exceed the rated safe working load of the winch by more than 10%.

requirements.title: Communications Loss Hold and Return-to-Home Response
requirements.description: The Offshore Survey USV-ROV shall, upon detection of a loss of the ROC communications link lasting more than 120 seconds, autonomously command the USV to maintain station and the ROV to hold depth, and shall initiate a return-to-surface and return-to-home sequence for both vehicles if the link is not restored within a further operator-configurable timeout period of between 5 and 60 minutes.
requirements.rationale: Loss of the satellite link is a credible and foreseeable failure mode at 50–200 km offshore; a defined hold-then-return behaviour is required to prevent vehicle loss and to satisfy the classification society safety case for unmanned autonomous operation.
acceptanceCriteria: In a hardware-in-the-loop test, when the ROC link is severed for 120 seconds, the autonomy system issues a station-keep command to the USV and a hold-depth command to the ROV within 5 seconds of the 120-second threshold being reached.

When the link remains severed beyond the configured timeout (set to 10 minutes for the test), both vehicles autonomously execute the return-to-home sequence and the ROV surfaces within 15 minutes of sequence initiation.

requirements.title: COLREGS-Compliant Collision Avoidance Detection Range
requirements.description: The Offshore Survey USV-ROV shall detect surface vessels and stationary obstacles at a minimum range of 2000 m and shall initiate a COLREGS-compliant avoidance manoeuvre with sufficient time to avoid collision at the USV's maximum transit speed.
requirements.rationale: COLREGS compliance is a mandatory regulatory requirement for autonomous surface vessels operating in international waters; a 2000 m detection range provides the minimum time margin for a give-way manoeuvre at 10 knots and is required by the classification society unmanned vessel notation.
acceptanceCriteria: During sea trials, the collision avoidance system detects a target vessel of ≥ 10 m length at a range of ≥ 2000 m in sea states up to SS4, verified by radar or AIS cross-reference, in at least 95% of 20 test encounters.
The system initiates an avoidance manoeuvre command within 10 seconds of detection when the predicted closest point of approach is less than 500 m.

requirements.title: Onboard Edge Processing Compression for Satellite Uplink
requirements.description: The Offshore Survey USV-ROV shall compress and prioritise combined ROV video and sonar payload data to a total uplink data rate not exceeding 50 Mbps before transmission to the ROC via the satellite link.
requirements.rationale: The LEO satellite link provides approximately 50 Mbps uplink capacity; without onboard compression of the estimated 500 Mbps raw sensor data stream, real-time payload delivery to the ROC is not feasible and mission value is lost.
acceptanceCriteria: During a representative survey mission simulation, the total data rate presented to the satellite terminal by the edge processing subsystem does not exceed 50 Mbps averaged over any 60-second window, verified by network traffic monitoring at the IF-002 interface.

Compressed HD video delivered to the ROC is assessed by a qualified operator as sufficient for habitat identification purposes, achieving a minimum MOS (Mean Opinion Score) of 3.5 out of 5 in a structured evaluation.

requirements.title: LARS Winch Rated Safe Working Load
requirements.description: The Launch & Recovery System shall provide a winch with a minimum rated safe working load of 275 kg in air, sufficient to handle the maximum ROV handling mass with a minimum safety factor of 1.1 applied.
requirements.rationale: The winch is the primary load-bearing element during ROV launch and recovery; a rated SWL of 275 kg (10% margin above the 250 kg maximum ROV handling mass) ensures the winch is not operated at its design limit during normal operations and provides headroom for dynamic loading in SS4 conditions as required by REQ-003.
acceptanceCriteria: The winch manufacturer's certificate of conformity states a rated safe working load of ≥ 275 kg, verified by inspection of the certification documentation.

A static proof load test at 1.5× SWL (≥ 412.5 kg) is completed without permanent deformation or failure of the winch drum, brake, or wire rope termination, in accordance with the applicable classification society lifting appliance standard.

requirements.title: LARS Motion Compensation Residual Velocity Limit
requirements.description: The Launch & Recovery System shall limit the residual vertical velocity of the ROV at the point of water entry or exit to no more than 0.5 m/s relative to the sea surface, when operating in sea states up to and including Sea State 4 (Hs ≤ 2.5 m).
requirements.rationale: Uncompensated heave motion of the USV in SS4 can produce vertical velocities exceeding 1.5 m/s at the stern ramp; limiting residual velocity to 0.5 m/s at water entry/exit is required to prevent impact damage to the ROV, tether, and LARS structure, and is the primary performance criterion for the motion compensation or active heave compensation system.
acceptanceCriteria: During LARS sea trials in measured SS4 conditions (Hs ≤ 2.5 m), the residual vertical velocity of the ROV handling point at water entry and exit does not exceed 0.5 m/s in 95% of measured events across a minimum of 10 launch-and-recovery cycles, as recorded by an inertial measurement unit mounted on the ROV handling frame.
The motion compensation system response time from heave sensor input to actuator correction does not exceed 100 ms, verified by hardware-in-the-loop testing against a recorded SS4 heave time-series.

requirements.title: Tether Management System Minimum Usable Tether Length
requirements.description: The Launch & Recovery System tether management system shall store and deploy a minimum of 1200 m of fibre-optic management tether without inducing tether twist, kinking, or bend radius violations below the tether manufacturer's minimum bend radius specification.
requirements.rationale: A 1200 m tether length is required to reach the maximum ROV operating depth of 1000 m with 200 m of operational slack to accommodate horizontal offset and current-induced catenary; the TMS must protect the tether's optical fibres and power conductors from mechanical damage throughout the full deployment and recovery cycle.
acceptanceCriteria: The TMS successfully completes 50 consecutive full-length deployment and recovery cycles (0 m to 1200 m and return) on a test rig without any measured tether twist accumulation exceeding 5° per metre of tether length, verified by twist markers at 100 m intervals.

Optical time-domain reflectometry (OTDR) measurements on all fibre channels show no increase in attenuation greater than 0.1 dB after the 50-cycle endurance test, confirming no bend-induced fibre damage.

interfaces.name: ROV Tether Power & Data
interfaces.description: Carries high-voltage DC power (5–20 kW) and bidirectional fibre-optic data between the USV Power Generation subsystem and the ROV Vehicle via the management tether. Power flows from the USV to the ROV; video, sonar, telemetry, and control signals flow bidirectionally over the fibre.

interfaces.name: Processed Payload & Command Relay
interfaces.description: Carries compressed and prioritised survey payload data (video, sonar) from the Autonomy & Control edge processor to the Communications subsystem for uplink to the ROC, and relays operator commands and mission updates received from the ROC back to the Autonomy subsystem.

history.author: ai
history.summary: Decomposed Offshore Survey USV-ROV system into 6 major subsystems with 2 primary interfaces
history.prompt:
Decompose the Offshore Survey USV-ROV system into its major subsystems. Create 4-7 subsystems based on this system description:

The system is a hybrid diesel-electric Unmanned Surface Vessel (USV) and tethered light work-class Remotely Operated Vehicle (ROV), operated in concert from a shore-based Remote Operations Centre (ROC) for offshore benthic habitat and environmental survey missions at ranges of 50–200 km from shore. The USV serves as the primary sea-surface platform, providing propulsion, power generation, a stern-ramp Launch and Recovery System (LARS) for the ROV, and a communications relay node between the ROV and the ROC. The ROC provides all mission planning, supervisory control, and payload data reception. The USV operates under supervised autonomy — executing operator-defined waypoints while autonomously managing station-keeping, collision avoidance, and fault responses — with the primary long-range link to the ROC provided by a LEO satellite constellation (e.g. Starlink or Iridium) at approximately 40–80 ms latency. The system is subject to full classification society oversight [assumed: DNV or Lloyd's Register unmanned vessel notation], which governs the rigour of the collision avoidance system, safety case, and design documentation programme.

The ROV is a light work-class vehicle (50–200 kg) deployed via the stern ramp on a fibre-optic management tether [assumed: tether carries high-voltage power and bidirectional data], capable of operating to depths of 300–1000 m. sections.title: Purpose & Scope
sections.content: The Offshore Survey USV-ROV is a hybrid diesel-electric unmanned surface vessel paired with a tethered light work-class ROV, designed to conduct benthic habitat and environmental survey missions at 50–200 km offshore without personnel at sea. The USV acts as the sea-surface host platform and communications relay, while the ROV performs subsea survey to depths of 300–1000 m. All mission planning, supervisory control, and payload data reception are handled from a shore-based Remote Operations Centre (ROC). The system is subject to classification society oversight (DNV or Lloyd's Register unmanned vessel notation), establishing the regulatory boundary for the safety case and design documentation programme.

sections.title: Capabilities
sections.content: The USV transits at up to 10 knots in sea states up to SS6 (Hs ~6 m) and maintains station during ROV operations under supervised autonomy, executing operator-defined waypoints with autonomous collision avoidance at a minimum detection range of 2,000 m. The ROV (estimated 120 kg in air) operates to 1,000 m depth on a 1,200 m fibre-optic management tether, receiving 5–20 kW of HVDC power and carrying HD cameras, multibeam/imaging sonar, and sediment sampling equipment. Onboard edge processing compresses approximately 500 Mbps of raw sensor data to fit within the ~50 Mbps satellite uplink budget before transmission to the ROC. On communications loss, both vehicles autonomously hold position and execute a return-to-home sequence after a defined timeout.

sections.title: Architecture Summary
sections.content: The system is decomposed into six subsystems. The USV Platform (SUB-USV) provides the hull, propulsion, and station-keeping foundation. Power Generation & Distribution (SUB-PWR) supplies a hybrid diesel-electric architecture with an estimated 60 kW generation capacity and 80 kWh battery bank, feeding both vessel hotel loads (~15 kW) and the ROV tether (~12 kW nominal). The ROV Vehicle (SUB-ROV) is the subsea survey element. The Launch & Recovery System (SUB-LARS) provides the stern-ramp mechanism rated to SS4 and capable of handling ROVs up to 250 kg. Communications & Data Link (SUB-COMMS) manages the LEO satellite primary link and backup 4G/LTE or VSAT links. Autonomy, Control & Mission Payload (SUB-AUTO) integrates navigation, collision avoidance, fault management, and edge data processing across all subsystems.

sections.title: Interface Summary
sections.content: Two primary system-level interfaces have been defined. IF-001 (ROV Tether Power & Data) carries HVDC power from SUB-PWR to the ROV and bidirectional fibre-optic data including HD video, sonar streams, 100 Hz control commands, and 10 Hz ROV telemetry. IF-002 (Processed Payload & Command Relay) is an Ethernet/IP link between SUB-AUTO and SUB-COMMS, carrying compressed video and sonar data for satellite uplink alongside inbound ROC operator commands and combined USV/ROV telemetry at 1 Hz. Additional internal interfaces between SUB-AUTO and propulsion, LARS sequencing, and navigation sensors remain to be formally defined.

sections.title: Constraints & Limitations
sections.content: ROV launch and recovery is restricted to SS4 (Hs ≤ 2.5 m); operations in higher sea states require the ROV to hold depth or abort to surface. The LEO satellite link imposes a nominal 40–80 ms one-way latency and an uplink cap of approximately 50 Mbps, requiring onboard compression to be effective before any real-time ROV piloting is feasible. Classification society scope (DNV/LR unmanned notation) constrains the collision avoidance system design, cybersecurity architecture, and the extent of the safety case — the precise scope covering LARS, ROV, and ROC has not yet been confirmed. The system must comply with COLREGS for autonomous surface navigation, which places minimum performance requirements on the collision avoidance detection range and response time.

sections.title: Open Issues & Risks
sections.content: Three architectural parameters remain unresolved and represent the highest-priority design decisions. First, mission endurance per deployment has not been defined, leaving fuel tankage, battery sizing, and autonomous energy management requirements unconstrained. Second, the classification society scope is unconfirmed — whether it extends beyond the USV hull to cover the LARS, ROV, and ROC cybersecurity architecture will materially affect the design documentation programme and cost. Third, the operational logistics model (fixed shore base vs. support vessel vs. hybrid) determines transit endurance requirements and the degree of autonomous self-sufficiency needed between deployments. Additionally, the specific ROV payload configuration (camera types, sonar model, sediment sampler) has not been confirmed, which affects tether power sizing and edge processing compression ratios. Internal interfaces between SUB-AUTO and propulsion/LARS/navigation sensors have not yet been formally defined.

after.title: USV Transit Speed
after.description: The Offshore Survey USV-ROV shall achieve a sustained transit speed of not less than 10 knots in sea states up to and including Sea State 4 (significant wave height Hs ≤ 2.5 m).
after.rationale: A minimum transit speed of 10 knots is required to reach survey sites 50–200 km offshore within an operationally acceptable transit window and to maintain schedule across a range of typical North Sea or equivalent sea conditions.
acceptanceCriteria: The USV sustains ≥ 10 knots over a 30-minute measured run in SS4 conditions (Hs ≤ 2.5 m) during sea trials.
Speed is measured as GPS-derived speed over ground averaged over the trial run, with no more than ±0.5 knot variation.

after.title: ROV Operating Depth
after.description: The Offshore Survey USV-ROV shall deploy the ROV to a maximum operating depth of 1000 m below mean sea level.
after.rationale: The benthic habitat survey mission requires access to continental shelf and upper slope environments; a 1000 m depth rating covers the full intended survey envelope and aligns with the light work-class ROV specification.
acceptanceCriteria: The ROV pressure housing and all penetrators are rated and tested to 1000 m (100 bar) in accordance with the applicable classification society pressure test standard.
The ROV successfully completes a functional dive to 1000 m depth during acceptance trials, with all payload systems operational at depth.

after.title: LARS Sea State Operational Limit
after.description: The Offshore Survey USV-ROV shall complete ROV launch and recovery operations via the stern-ramp LARS in sea states up to and including Sea State 4 (significant wave height Hs ≤ 2.5 m).
after.rationale: Safe ROV handling at the sea surface is the primary operational constraint on mission availability; SS4 is the accepted industry limit for light work-class ROV LARS operations and is required to achieve acceptable mission uptime in the target operating area.
acceptanceCriteria: The LARS successfully completes a minimum of 3 consecutive launch-and-recovery cycles with the ROV at its maximum handling mass (250 kg) in simulated or measured SS4 conditions (Hs ≤ 2.5 m) without damage to the ROV, tether, or LARS structure.
Peak dynamic load on the LARS winch during SS4 trials does not exceed the rated safe working load of the winch by more than 10%.

after.title: Communications Loss Hold and Return-to-Home Response
after.description: The Offshore Survey USV-ROV shall, upon detection of a loss of the ROC communications link lasting more than 120 seconds, autonomously command the USV to maintain station and the ROV to hold depth, and shall initiate a return-to-surface and return-to-home sequence for both vehicles if the link is not restored within a further operator-configurable timeout period of between 5 and 60 minutes.
after.rationale: Loss of the satellite link is a credible and foreseeable failure mode at 50–200 km offshore; a defined hold-then-return behaviour is required to prevent vehicle loss and to satisfy the classification society safety case for unmanned autonomous operation.
acceptanceCriteria: In a hardware-in-the-loop test, when the ROC link is severed for 120 seconds, the autonomy system issues a station-keep command to the USV and a hold-depth command to the ROV within 5 seconds of the 120-second threshold being reached.

When the link remains severed beyond the configured timeout (set to 10 minutes for the test), both vehicles autonomously execute the return-to-home sequence and the ROV surfaces within 15 minutes of sequence initiation.

after.title: COLREGS-Compliant Collision Avoidance Detection Range
after.description: The Offshore Survey USV-ROV shall detect surface vessels and stationary obstacles at a minimum range of 2000 m and shall initiate a COLREGS-compliant avoidance manoeuvre with sufficient time to avoid collision at the USV's maximum transit speed.
after.rationale: COLREGS compliance is a mandatory regulatory requirement for autonomous surface vessels operating in international waters; a 2000 m detection range provides the minimum time margin for a give-way manoeuvre at 10 knots and is required by the classification society unmanned vessel notation.
acceptanceCriteria: During sea trials, the collision avoidance system detects a target vessel of ≥ 10 m length at a range of ≥ 2000 m in sea states up to SS4, verified by radar or AIS cross-reference, in at least 95% of 20 test encounters.
The system initiates an avoidance manoeuvre command within 10 seconds of detection when the predicted closest point of approach is less than 500 m.

after.title: Onboard Edge Processing Compression for Satellite Uplink
after.description: The Offshore Survey USV-ROV shall compress and prioritise combined ROV video and sonar payload data to a total uplink data rate not exceeding 50 Mbps before transmission to the ROC via the satellite link.
after.rationale: The LEO satellite link provides approximately 50 Mbps uplink capacity; without onboard compression of the estimated 500 Mbps raw sensor data stream, real-time payload delivery to the ROC is not feasible and mission value is lost.
acceptanceCriteria: During a representative survey mission simulation, the total data rate presented to the satellite terminal by the edge processing subsystem does not exceed 50 Mbps averaged over any 60-second window, verified by network traffic monitoring at the IF-002 interface.

Compressed HD video delivered to the ROC is assessed by a qualified operator as sufficient for habitat identification purposes, achieving a minimum MOS (Mean Opinion Score) of 3.5 out of 5 in a structured evaluation.

after.title: LARS Winch Rated Safe Working Load
after.description: The Launch & Recovery System shall provide a winch with a minimum rated safe working load of 275 kg in air, sufficient to handle the maximum ROV handling mass with a minimum safety factor of 1.1 applied.
after.rationale: The winch is the primary load-bearing element during ROV launch and recovery; a rated SWL of 275 kg (10% margin above the 250 kg maximum ROV handling mass) ensures the winch is not operated at its design limit during normal operations and provides headroom for dynamic loading in SS4 conditions as required by REQ-003.
acceptanceCriteria: The winch manufacturer's certificate of conformity states a rated safe working load of ≥ 275 kg, verified by inspection of the certification documentation.

A static proof load test at 1.5× SWL (≥ 412.5 kg) is completed without permanent deformation or failure of the winch drum, brake, or wire rope termination, in accordance with the applicable classification society lifting appliance standard.

after.title: LARS Motion Compensation Residual Velocity Limit
after.description: The Launch & Recovery System shall limit the residual vertical velocity of the ROV at the point of water entry or exit to no more than 0.5 m/s relative to the sea surface, when operating in sea states up to and including Sea State 4 (Hs ≤ 2.5 m).
after.rationale: Uncompensated heave motion of the USV in SS4 can produce vertical velocities exceeding 1.5 m/s at the stern ramp; limiting residual velocity to 0.5 m/s at water entry/exit is required to prevent impact damage to the ROV, tether, and LARS structure, and is the primary performance criterion for the motion compensation or active heave compensation system.
acceptanceCriteria: During LARS sea trials in measured SS4 conditions (Hs ≤ 2.5 m), the residual vertical velocity of the ROV handling point at water entry and exit does not exceed 0.5 m/s in 95% of measured events across a minimum of 10 launch-and-recovery cycles, as recorded by an inertial measurement unit mounted on the ROV handling frame.
The motion compensation system response time from heave sensor input to actuator correction does not exceed 100 ms, verified by hardware-in-the-loop testing against a recorded SS4 heave time-series.

after.title: Tether Management System Minimum Usable Tether Length
after.description: The Launch & Recovery System tether management system shall store and deploy a minimum of 1200 m of fibre-optic management tether without inducing tether twist, kinking, or bend radius violations below the tether manufacturer's minimum bend radius specification.
after.rationale: A 1200 m tether length is required to reach the maximum ROV operating depth of 1000 m with 200 m of operational slack to accommodate horizontal offset and current-induced catenary; the TMS must protect the tether's optical fibres and power conductors from mechanical damage throughout the full deployment and recovery cycle.
acceptanceCriteria: The TMS successfully completes 50 consecutive full-length deployment and recovery cycles (0 m to 1200 m and return) on a test rig without any measured tether twist accumulation exceeding 5° per metre of tether length, verified by twist markers at 100 m intervals.

Optical time-domain reflectometry (OTDR) measurements on all fibre channels show no increase in attenuation greater than 0.1 dB after the 50-cycle endurance test, confirming no bend-induced fibre damage.

sections.title: Purpose & Scope
sections.content: The ROV Vehicle subsystem is the subsea survey element of the Offshore Survey USV-ROV system, providing a tethered, remotely operated platform capable of conducting benthic habitat and environmental surveys at depths of 300–1000 m. It exists to extend the system's operational reach below the sea surface, carrying scientific payload to the seabed while the USV remains at the surface as host and relay. The ROV operates under continuous supervisory control from the shore-based ROC, with all power and bidirectional data exchanged via a fibre-optic management tether deployed and recovered by the LARS subsystem. Its scope covers the vehicle structure, thrusters, pressure housings, onboard electronics, and integrated survey payload.

sections.title: Capabilities
sections.content: The ROV is a light work-class vehicle with an estimated in-air mass of 120 kg (within the 50–200 kg class envelope) and a pressure rating of 1000 m (100 bar), satisfying REQ-002. It receives 12 kW nominal HVDC power via the management tether (range 5–20 kW) and consumes approximately 10 kW at survey speed across hotel loads and thrusters. The survey payload suite comprises HD video cameras, multibeam or imaging sonar, and sediment sampling equipment, generating an estimated 500 Mbps of raw data that is forwarded to the USV edge processor for compression before satellite uplink. The vehicle supports 100 Hz control command ingestion and 10 Hz telemetry output over the fibre-optic data channel.

sections.title: Architecture Summary
sections.content: The ROV Vehicle subsystem has not yet been formally decomposed into child assemblies; the current model treats it as a single subsystem node within the system hierarchy. Functionally, the vehicle integrates a thruster array for six-degree-of-freedom manoeuvring, a pressure-rated electronics housing for control and payload electronics, a fibre-optic tether termination and power conversion unit, and the survey payload bay. The tether connects the ROV to the LARS tether management system (SUB-LARS) topside, which manages deployment, recovery, and tether storage. Decomposition into assemblies — such as vehicle frame, propulsion, electronics housing, and payload bay — is a recommended next step as the design matures.

sections.title: Interface Summary
sections.content: The ROV Vehicle has one formally defined system-level interface: IF-001 (ROV Tether Power & Data), a combined HVDC power and fibre-optic data link sourced from SUB-PWR. This interface carries continuous HVDC power to the ROV, continuous HD video and sonar data streams from the ROV to the USV, 100 Hz control commands from the USV to the ROV, and 10 Hz ROV telemetry in return. A physical handling interface with SUB-LARS exists during launch and recovery operations — the LARS stern ramp, winch, and tether management system engage the ROV directly — but this interface has not yet been formally defined as an IF entity. No direct electrical or data interface between the ROV and SUB-COMMS or SUB-AUTO is defined; all data routing is mediated by SUB-PWR and SUB-AUTO topside.

sections.title: Constraints & Limitations
sections.content: The ROV is depth-rated to 1000 m; all pressure housings and penetrators must be tested to 100 bar in accordance with the applicable classification society standard (REQ-002). Vehicle mass must remain within the LARS maximum handling capacity of 250 kg in air (REQ-003, REQ-007), providing a 130 kg margin above the current 120 kg estimate for payload growth. Launch and recovery are restricted to SS4 (Hs ≤ 2.5 m) by the LARS operational limit; in higher sea states the ROV must hold depth or abort to surface. The tether length is constrained to 1200 m maximum by the LARS tether management system (REQ-009), which sets the practical operational depth limit when accounting for current-induced catenary offset. The specific payload configuration — camera types, sonar model, and sediment sampler — has not been confirmed, leaving tether power sizing and raw data rate estimates subject to revision.

sections.title: Verification Status
sections.content: Two system-level requirements directly govern the ROV Vehicle: REQ-002 (ROV Operating Depth, 1000 m) and the tether length constraint embedded in REQ-009. Neither has an associated verification record defined at this stage; both are expected to be verified by test during acceptance trials — REQ-002 via a functional dive to 1000 m with all payload systems operational, and REQ-009 via OTDR fibre attenuation measurement after a 50-cycle tether endurance test on the LARS rig. No VER- entities have been created for SUB-ROV requirements, representing a gap in the verification coverage that should be addressed as the design progresses.

sections.title: Open Issues & Risks
sections.content: The ROV payload configuration (camera specification, sonar model, sediment sampler type) remains unconfirmed, which directly affects tether power budget, raw data rate, and the compression ratio required from the SUB-AUTO edge processor — this is the highest-priority open item for the ROV subsystem. The physical LARS-to-ROV handling interface has not been formally defined as an interface entity, leaving the mechanical and structural coupling between the two subsystems undocumented. The ROV subsystem has not been decomposed into child assemblies, meaning no mass, power, or volume budgets have been allocated at the assembly level. Additionally, the classification society scope — whether it extends to the ROV pressure housings and electronics — has not been confirmed, which may impose additional design and documentation requirements on the vehicle.

before.name: Offshore Survey USV-ROV
before.description:
The system is a hybrid diesel-electric Unmanned Surface Vessel (USV) and tethered light work-class Remotely Operated Vehicle (ROV), operated in concert from a shore-based Remote Operations Centre (ROC) for offshore benthic habitat and environmental survey missions at ranges of 50–200 km from shore. The USV serves as the primary sea-surface platform, providing propulsion, power generation, a stern-ramp Launch and Recovery System (LARS) for the ROV, and a communications relay node between the ROV and the ROC. The ROC provides all mission planning, supervisory control, and payload data reception. The USV operates under supervised autonomy — executing operator-defined waypoints while autonomously managing station-keeping, collision avoidance, and fault responses — with the primary long-range link to the ROC provided by a LEO satellite constellation (e.g. Starlink or Iridium) at approximately 40–80 ms latency. The system is subject to full classification society oversight [assumed: DNV or Lloyd's Register unmanned vessel notation], which governs the rigour of the collision avoidance system, safety case, and design documentation programme.

The ROV is a light work-class vehicle (50–200 kg) deployed via the stern ramp on a fibre-optic management tether [assumed: tether carries high-voltage power and bidirectional data], capable of operating to depths of 300–1000 m. Its primary mission payload is seabed habitat and benthic survey, comprising high-definition video cameras, multibeam or imaging sonar, and sediment sampling equipment [specific payload configuration to be confirmed]. Onboard edge processing on the USV compresses and prioritises video and sonar data before uplink to the ROC, managing the bandwidth constraints of the satellite link. The USV is designed to transit in sea states up to Sea State 6 (Hs ~6 m), with ROV launch and recovery via the stern ramp restricted to Sea State 4 (Hs ~2.5 m) or below. In the event of a communications loss, both vehicles enter a hold state — the USV maintains station and the ROV holds depth — awaiting link restoration up to a defined timeout, after which both vehicles execute an autonomous return-to-surface and home sequence.

The USV power architecture is hybrid diesel-electric with significant battery capacity, sized to supply both vessel hotel loads and ROV tether power simultaneously [assumed: 5–20 kW delivered to ROV at operating depth via high-voltage DC tether]. Three architectural parameters remain open and will shape detailed design: (1) mission endurance per deployment, which drives fuel tankage, battery sizing, and autonomous energy management requirements; (2) the classification society scope — whether it covers the USV only or extends to the LARS, ROV, and ROC cybersecurity architecture; and (3) the operational logistics model — whether the USV operates from a fixed shore base, a support vessel, or a hybrid of both — which determines transit endurance requirements and the degree of autonomous self-sufficiency required between deployments.

sections.title: Purpose & Scope
sections.content: The Offshore Survey USV-ROV is a hybrid diesel-electric unmanned surface vessel paired with a tethered light work-class ROV, designed to conduct benthic habitat and environmental survey missions at 50–200 km offshore without personnel at sea. The USV acts as the sea-surface host platform and communications relay, while the ROV performs subsea survey to depths of 300–1000 m. All mission planning, supervisory control, and payload data reception are handled from a shore-based Remote Operations Centre (ROC). The system is subject to classification society oversight (DNV or Lloyd's Register unmanned vessel notation), establishing the regulatory boundary for the safety case and design documentation programme.

sections.title: Capabilities
sections.content: The USV transits at up to 10 knots in sea states up to SS6 (Hs ~6 m) and maintains station during ROV operations under supervised autonomy, executing operator-defined waypoints with autonomous collision avoidance at a minimum detection range of 2,000 m. The ROV (estimated 120 kg in air) operates to 1,000 m depth on a 1,200 m fibre-optic management tether, receiving 5–20 kW of HVDC power and carrying HD cameras, multibeam/imaging sonar, and sediment sampling equipment. Onboard edge processing compresses approximately 500 Mbps of raw sensor data to fit within the ~50 Mbps satellite uplink budget before transmission to the ROC. On communications loss, both vehicles autonomously hold position and execute a return-to-home sequence after a defined timeout.

sections.title: Architecture Summary
sections.content: The system is decomposed into six subsystems. The USV Platform (SUB-USV) provides the hull, propulsion, and station-keeping foundation. Power Generation & Distribution (SUB-PWR) supplies a hybrid diesel-electric architecture with an estimated 60 kW generation capacity and 80 kWh battery bank, feeding both vessel hotel loads (~15 kW) and the ROV tether (~12 kW nominal). The ROV Vehicle (SUB-ROV) is the subsea survey element. The Launch & Recovery System (SUB-LARS) provides the stern-ramp mechanism rated to SS4 and capable of handling ROVs up to 250 kg. Communications & Data Link (SUB-COMMS) manages the LEO satellite primary link and backup 4G/LTE or VSAT links. Autonomy, Control & Mission Payload (SUB-AUTO) integrates navigation, collision avoidance, fault management, and edge data processing across all subsystems.

sections.title: Interface Summary
sections.content: Two primary system-level interfaces have been defined. IF-001 (ROV Tether Power & Data) carries HVDC power from SUB-PWR to the ROV and bidirectional fibre-optic data including HD video, sonar streams, 100 Hz control commands, and 10 Hz ROV telemetry. IF-002 (Processed Payload & Command Relay) is an Ethernet/IP link between SUB-AUTO and SUB-COMMS, carrying compressed video and sonar data for satellite uplink alongside inbound ROC operator commands and combined USV/ROV telemetry at 1 Hz. Additional internal interfaces between SUB-AUTO and propulsion, LARS sequencing, and navigation sensors remain to be formally defined.

sections.title: Constraints & Limitations
sections.content: ROV launch and recovery is restricted to SS4 (Hs ≤ 2.5 m); operations in higher sea states require the ROV to hold depth or abort to surface. The LEO satellite link imposes a nominal 40–80 ms one-way latency and an uplink cap of approximately 50 Mbps, requiring onboard compression to be effective before any real-time ROV piloting is feasible. Classification society scope (DNV/LR unmanned notation) constrains the collision avoidance system design, cybersecurity architecture, and the extent of the safety case — the precise scope covering LARS, ROV, and ROC has not yet been confirmed. The system must comply with COLREGS for autonomous surface navigation, which places minimum performance requirements on the collision avoidance detection range and response time.

sections.title: Open Issues & Risks
sections.content: Three architectural parameters remain unresolved and represent the highest-priority design decisions. First, mission endurance per deployment has not been defined, leaving fuel tankage, battery sizing, and autonomous energy management requirements unconstrained. Second, the classification society scope is unconfirmed — whether it extends beyond the USV hull to cover the LARS, ROV, and ROC cybersecurity architecture will materially affect the design documentation programme and cost. Third, the operational logistics model (fixed shore base vs. support vessel vs. hybrid) determines transit endurance requirements and the degree of autonomous self-sufficiency needed between deployments. Additionally, the specific ROV payload configuration (camera types, sonar model, sediment sampler) has not been confirmed, which affects tether power sizing and edge processing compression ratios. Internal interfaces between SUB-AUTO and propulsion/LARS/navigation sensors have not yet been formally defined.
