_narrativeAnalysis.mainstaySentence: The Power Generation & Distribution energises and commands the ROV Vehicle, whose video and sonar returns the Autonomy, Control & Mission Payload edge-processes and compresses, which the Communications & Data Link relays as processed survey data to the remote operations centre ashore.

cards.title: The Survey Chain: Power to Insight
cards.body: The central purpose of the Offshore Survey USV-ROV is to collect high-fidelity subsea environmental data and deliver it, in near-real time, to shore-based operators. That purpose is served by a two-link mainstay. First, Power Generation & Distribution drives the tether, pushing HVDC power down to the ROV Vehicle while simultaneously carrying control commands and receiving raw video and sonar data back up the same fibre-optic core. Second, Autonomy, Control & Mission Payload ingests those raw streams, applies onboard edge processing, and hands compressed, mission-ready data to Communications & Data Link for transmission to the remote operations centre. Every other subsystem in the architecture exists to keep this chain intact and productive.

cards.title: The Tether Interface: Power and Perception in One Bundle
cards.body: Interface IF-001 is the physical and logical spine of the system. Power Generation & Distribution does not merely supply electricity to the ROV Vehicle — it also acts as the conduit for bidirectional mission data. HVDC power flows down the tether to sustain the ROV's thrusters, lights, and sensors, while rov_control_commands issued by the autonomy layer travel the same path to steer the vehicle. In return, rov_video_sonar_data and rov_telemetry ascend the fibre-optic core, making the tether simultaneously a power umbilical and a high-bandwidth sensor highway. This tight coupling means that any interruption to Power Generation & Distribution is an immediate threat not just to propulsion but to the entire survey data pipeline.

cards.title: Autonomy and Communications: Processing Before Transmission
cards.body: Interface IF-002 marks the boundary between raw data and actionable intelligence. Autonomy, Control & Mission Payload performs onboard edge processing — compressing video, filtering sonar returns, and fusing USV and ROV telemetry — before handing the resulting streams to Communications & Data Link. This architecture deliberately reduces the bandwidth burden on the long-range link: compressed_video_stream and compressed_sonar_data are transmission-ready products, not raw sensor dumps. Equally important, the interface is bidirectional: roc_operator_commands flow inbound from the shore-based remote operations centre through Communications & Data Link to the autonomy layer, allowing supervised override of autonomous behaviours without requiring a direct operator-to-ROV link.

cards.title: Narrative Islands: USV Platform and LARS
cards.body: Two subsystems — USV Platform and Launch & Recovery System — currently appear as narrative islands: they carry no modelled interfaces in this graph. This does not mean they are unimportant; on the contrary, USV Platform provides the hull, propulsion, and station-keeping that physically positions the entire system over the survey site, and Launch & Recovery System enables the safe deployment and retrieval of the ROV Vehicle in sea states up to SS4. Their absence from the interface graph indicates a modelling gap rather than a functional one. Future iterations should capture, at minimum, the power draw of USV Platform from Power Generation & Distribution, the mechanical and control signals between LARS and Autonomy, Control & Mission Payload, and the navigation data exchange between USV Platform and the autonomy layer.

genesis.originalDescription: An unscrewed surface vessel with a subsea ROV that can be launched from it, all controlled from a remote operation centre on shore.

questions.question: What is the primary mission or application for this system?
questions.context: The mission drives almost everything: payload requirements, endurance, navigation precision, and the ROV's sensor suite. A pipeline inspection system looks very different from a search-and-rescue or environmental monitoring system.
options: Subsea infrastructure inspection (pipelines, cables, structures)
Environmental monitoring / survey
Search and rescue / object recovery
Military / security surveillance
Scientific research

questions.question: What is the intended operational range and endurance of the USV from the shore-based control centre?
questions.context: Range determines the communications architecture — whether you can rely on line-of-sight radio, need satellite comms, or require an autonomous fallback mode. Endurance drives power and fuel system sizing.
options: Nearshore / harbour operations (< 5 km)
Coastal operations (5–50 km)
Offshore operations (50–200 km)
Deep offshore / open ocean (200 km+)

questions.question: What level of autonomy is expected for the USV during transit and station-keeping while the ROV is deployed?
questions.context: This shapes the USV's onboard intelligence and sensor suite. A fully teleoperated USV needs a high-bandwidth, low-latency comms link at all times, whereas an autonomous USV needs onboard collision avoidance, path planning, and fault management.
options: Fully teleoperated (operator drives every manoeuvre)
Supervised autonomy (operator sets waypoints, USV handles station-keeping and collision avoidance)
High autonomy (USV executes full mission plans with minimal operator input)
questions.answer: Supervised autonomy (operator sets waypoints, USV handles station-keeping and collision avoidance)

questions.question: How is the ROV physically integrated with the USV — specifically the launch and recovery system (LARS)?
questions.context: The LARS design is a major subsystem that affects the USV's structural layout, deck space, power budget, and operational sea-state limits. A simple davit/crane system is very different from a through-hull moonpool or a stern-ramp deployment system.
options: Crane or davit over the side/stern
Stern ramp / slipway
Moonpool (through-hull deployment)
Not yet defined

questions.question: What is the communications architecture between the ROV and the shore-based control centre — does the ROV tether connect back to the USV, and how does data then reach shore?
questions.context: The ROV-to-USV link (typically a fibre-optic tether) and the USV-to-shore link (RF, satellite, or 4G/5G) are two distinct communication hops with very different bandwidth and latency characteristics. Understanding this chain is critical to defining the system interfaces.
options: ROV tethered to USV, USV relays all data to shore via RF/satellite
ROV tethered to USV, USV relays data, with onboard edge processing to reduce bandwidth
Untethered ROV with acoustic comms to USV, USV relays to shore
Not yet defined
questions.answer: ROV tethered to USV, USV relays data, with onboard edge processing to reduce bandwidth

rounds.refinedDescription:
The system is an unmanned maritime platform comprising an Unmanned Surface Vessel (USV) and a deployed Remotely Operated Vehicle (ROV), operated in concert from a shore-based Remote Operations Centre (ROC). The USV acts as the primary sea-surface asset: providing propulsion, power generation, communications relay, and a Launch and Recovery System (LARS) for the ROV. The ROC provides all supervisory control, mission planning, and payload data reception for both vehicles.

The system operates across two physical domains simultaneously: the USV navigates the sea surface while the ROV conducts subsea operations, with the two vehicles connected via a management tether [assumed: fibre-optic tether carrying power and data]. The ROC communicates with the USV via a long-range link [assumed: satellite or RF], and the USV in turn relays commands and data to/from the ROV. This creates a two-hop command-and-control chain that must be managed for latency, bandwidth, and resilience.

Key system constraints and interfaces are not yet fully defined, including the operational range, mission type, sea-state operating limits, and the degree of onboard autonomy on the USV. These factors will significantly shape the subsystem architecture, particularly the communications stack, the LARS design, and the onboard autonomy and fault-management capabilities of the USV.

questions.question: What environmental parameters is the system primarily designed to monitor, and where — water column, seabed, or both?
questions.context: This directly determines the ROV's sensor payload suite and therefore its size, power draw, and tether requirements. A water-column chemistry survey (CTD, water samplers) implies a very different ROV than a seabed habitat mapping mission (multibeam sonar, cameras, sediment samplers). It also affects whether the USV needs significant onboard sample or data storage.
options: Water column physical/chemical properties (temperature, salinity, dissolved oxygen, pH)
Seabed habitat and benthic survey (video, sonar, sediment sampling)
Both water column and seabed in a single deployment
Atmospheric / air-sea interface monitoring (with subsea as secondary)
questions.answer: Seabed habitat and benthic survey (video, sonar, sediment sampling)

questions.question: What is the expected maximum operating sea state for the USV, and what is the minimum sea state at which ROV launch and recovery via the stern ramp must remain safe?
questions.context: Sea state limits are the primary driver for USV hull sizing, LARS design complexity, and station-keeping thruster power. There is often a gap between the USV's transit capability and the more restrictive ROV launch/recovery window — defining both numbers now prevents a major design mismatch later.
options: USV: up to Sea State 5 (Hs ~3.5 m) / LARS: up to Sea State 3 (Hs ~1.25 m)
USV: up to Sea State 4 (Hs ~2.5 m) / LARS: up to Sea State 2 (Hs ~0.6 m)
USV: up to Sea State 6 (Hs ~6 m) / LARS: up to Sea State 4 (Hs ~2.5 m)
Not yet defined
questions.answer: USV: up to Sea State 6 (Hs ~6 m) / LARS: up to Sea State 4 (Hs ~2.5 m)

questions.question: What is the approximate size and weight class of the ROV, and what is the maximum tether length required for the deepest survey target?
questions.context: ROV weight class (observation-class ~50 kg vs. work-class ~1000 kg+) determines the LARS structural loads, the USV deck layout, and the tether drum size. Maximum operating depth sets tether length, which in turn affects tether drag, the USV's station-keeping thrust requirement, and the tether management system design.
options: Micro / observation class (< 50 kg, < 300 m depth)
Light work class (50–200 kg, 300–1000 m depth)
Standard work class (200–1000 kg, 1000–3000 m depth)
Not yet defined
questions.answer: Light work class (50–200 kg, 300–1000 m depth)

questions.question: What is the primary long-range communications link between the USV and the shore-based ROC?
questions.context: At 50–200 km offshore, line-of-sight RF is marginal or unavailable, making this a critical architecture decision. Satellite comms (e.g. VSAT, Iridium, Starlink) offer global coverage but introduce latency (600 ms+ for GEO) that affects ROV piloting feel. 4G/5G LTE has low latency but limited offshore range. The choice affects the supervisory autonomy requirements on the USV — higher latency demands more onboard decision-making.
options: VSAT / GEO satellite (high bandwidth, ~600 ms latency)
LEO satellite constellation e.g. Starlink / Iridium (moderate bandwidth, ~40–80 ms latency)
4G/5G LTE with offshore coverage (low latency, range-limited)
Hybrid — primary satellite with LTE inshore fallback
questions.answer: LEO satellite constellation e.g. Starlink / Iridium (moderate bandwidth, ~40–80 ms latency)

rounds.refinedDescription:
The system is an Unmanned Surface Vessel (USV) and tethered Remotely Operated Vehicle (ROV) operated in concert from a shore-based Remote Operations Centre (ROC) for offshore environmental monitoring and survey missions at ranges of 50–200 km from shore. The USV serves as the primary sea-surface platform, providing propulsion, power generation, a stern-ramp Launch and Recovery System (LARS) for the ROV, and a communications relay node between the ROV and the ROC. The ROC provides all mission planning, supervisory control, and payload data reception; the USV operates under supervised autonomy, executing operator-defined waypoints while autonomously managing station-keeping, collision avoidance, and fault responses.

The ROV is deployed via the stern ramp and connected to the USV by a management tether [assumed: fibre-optic tether carrying both power and bidirectional data]. The ROV conducts subsea environmental survey operations [payload suite to be confirmed], with onboard edge processing on the USV compressing and prioritising data before transmission to the ROC over a long-range communications link [type to be confirmed — likely satellite]. This two-hop command-and-control chain (ROC → USV → ROV) introduces latency and bandwidth constraints that will shape the autonomy architecture of both vehicles, particularly the USV's ability to maintain safe station-keeping and tether management without continuous operator input.

Key parameters still to be confirmed include the environmental monitoring payload (water column, seabed, or both), the ROV weight class and maximum operating depth, the sea-state operating envelope for both transit and LARS operations, and the long-range communications technology. These decisions will drive the USV hull sizing, LARS structural design, tether drum specification, onboard power budget, and the latency tolerance requirements for the supervisory control architecture.

questions.question: What is the USV's primary power source, and how is power delivered to the ROV via the tether?
questions.context: A light work-class ROV at up to 1000 m depth requires significant tether power — typically 5–20 kW delivered at high voltage (e.g. 3000 VDC) to minimise resistive losses over a long tether. The USV power architecture (diesel generator, hybrid diesel-electric, or battery) must be sized to supply both hotel loads and ROV power simultaneously. This also affects endurance and the USV's acoustic and emissions signature, which may matter for sensitive environmental survey work.
options: Diesel generator (primary) with battery buffer
Hybrid diesel-electric with significant battery capacity
Fully battery-electric (range/endurance limited)
Not yet defined
questions.answer: Hybrid diesel-electric with significant battery capacity

questions.question: Are there any regulatory or classification requirements the system must comply with, and does the USV need to operate in areas with other maritime traffic requiring COLREGS compliance?
questions.context: Regulatory framework shapes the entire autonomy and safety architecture. A system operating in open offshore waters with no traffic may have minimal certification burden, whereas one transiting busy coastal shipping lanes needs a certified collision avoidance system meeting COLREGS Rule 2 and potentially IMO or flag-state approval for unmanned operation. This also affects whether a safety case or formal hazard analysis is required.
options: Open offshore operations, minimal traffic — internal safety case sufficient
Mixed coastal and offshore — COLREGS compliance required, flag-state notification
Full classification society oversight required (e.g. DNV, Lloyd's unmanned vessel notation)
Not yet defined / regulatory landscape still being assessed
questions.answer: Full classification society oversight required (e.g. DNV, Lloyd's unmanned vessel notation)

questions.question: What happens when the communications link to the ROC is lost — what is the required safe-state behaviour for both the USV and the ROV?
questions.context: Comms loss handling is a top-level safety requirement that directly drives the onboard autonomy architecture. The USV must have a defined fallback: hold position, return to a safe waypoint, or continue the mission plan. The ROV must also have a defined response: hold depth, ascend to a safe depth, or surface. These decisions determine the complexity of the onboard autonomy stack and the watchdog/heartbeat systems required.
options: USV holds station; ROV holds depth — await comms restoration up to a defined timeout, then both return to surface/home
USV executes pre-loaded return-to-home waypoint; ROV ascends and is recovered automatically
USV continues current mission plan autonomously; ROV continues survey pattern
Not yet defined
questions.answer: USV holds station; ROV holds depth — await comms restoration up to a defined timeout, then both return to surface/home

rounds.refinedDescription:
The system is an Unmanned Surface Vessel (USV) and tethered Remotely Operated Vehicle (ROV) operated in concert from a shore-based Remote Operations Centre (ROC) for offshore environmental monitoring and benthic survey missions at ranges of 50–200 km from shore. The USV serves as the primary sea-surface platform, providing propulsion, power generation, a stern-ramp Launch and Recovery System (LARS) for the ROV, and a communications relay node between the ROV and the ROC. The ROC provides all mission planning, supervisory control, and payload data reception; the USV operates under supervised autonomy, executing operator-defined waypoints while autonomously managing station-keeping, collision avoidance, and fault responses. The primary long-range communications link between the USV and ROC uses a LEO satellite constellation (e.g. Starlink or Iridium), providing moderate bandwidth with approximately 40–80 ms latency — sufficient to support ROV piloting without the supervisory lag associated with GEO satellite systems.

The ROV is a light work-class vehicle (50–200 kg) deployed via the stern ramp on a fibre-optic management tether [assumed: tether carries both high-voltage power and bidirectional data], capable of operating to depths of 300–1000 m. Its primary mission payload is seabed habitat and benthic survey, comprising high-definition video cameras, multibeam or imaging sonar, and sediment sampling equipment [specific payload configuration to be confirmed]. Onboard edge processing on the USV compresses and prioritises video and sonar data before transmission to the ROC, managing the bandwidth constraints of the satellite link. The USV is designed to operate in sea states up to Sea State 6 (Hs ~6 m) for transit, with ROV launch and recovery via the stern ramp restricted to Sea State 4 (Hs ~2.5 m) or below.

Three significant architectural decisions remain open and will shape the detailed system design. First, the USV power architecture — whether diesel-generator, hybrid, or battery-electric — must be sized to supply both vessel hotel loads and ROV tether power (estimated 5–20 kW at depth) simultaneously, with implications for endurance and acoustic signature during sensitive survey operations. Second, the regulatory and classification framework governing unmanned offshore vessel operation must be established, as this determines the rigour of the collision avoidance system and the required safety case. Third, the comms-loss safe-state behaviour for both vehicles must be formally defined, as this is the primary driver for the complexity of the onboard autonomy and watchdog architecture.

genesis.finalDescription:
The system is a hybrid diesel-electric Unmanned Surface Vessel (USV) and tethered light work-class Remotely Operated Vehicle (ROV), operated in concert from a shore-based Remote Operations Centre (ROC) for offshore benthic habitat and environmental survey missions at ranges of 50–200 km from shore. The USV serves as the primary sea-surface platform, providing propulsion, power generation, a stern-ramp Launch and Recovery System (LARS) for the ROV, and a communications relay node between the ROV and the ROC. The ROC provides all mission planning, supervisory control, and payload data reception. The USV operates under supervised autonomy — executing operator-defined waypoints while autonomously managing station-keeping, collision avoidance, and fault responses — with the primary long-range link to the ROC provided by a LEO satellite constellation (e.g. Starlink or Iridium) at approximately 40–80 ms latency. The system is subject to full classification society oversight [assumed: DNV or Lloyd's Register unmanned vessel notation], which governs the rigour of the collision avoidance system, safety case, and design documentation programme.

The ROV is a light work-class vehicle (50–200 kg) deployed via the stern ramp on a fibre-optic management tether [assumed: tether carries high-voltage power and bidirectional data], capable of operating to depths of 300–1000 m. Its primary mission payload is seabed habitat and benthic survey, comprising high-definition video cameras, multibeam or imaging sonar, and sediment sampling equipment [specific payload configuration to be confirmed]. Onboard edge processing on the USV compresses and prioritises video and sonar data before uplink to the ROC, managing the bandwidth constraints of the satellite link. The USV is designed to transit in sea states up to Sea State 6 (Hs ~6 m), with ROV launch and recovery via the stern ramp restricted to Sea State 4 (Hs ~2.5 m) or below. In the event of a communications loss, both vehicles enter a hold state — the USV maintains station and the ROV holds depth — awaiting link restoration up to a defined timeout, after which both vehicles execute an autonomous return-to-surface and home sequence.

The USV power architecture is hybrid diesel-electric with significant battery capacity, sized to supply both vessel hotel loads and ROV tether power simultaneously [assumed: 5–20 kW delivered to ROV at operating depth via high-voltage DC tether]. Three architectural parameters remain open and will shape detailed design: (1) mission endurance per deployment, which drives fuel tankage, battery sizing, and autonomous energy management requirements; (2) the classification society scope — whether it covers the USV only or extends to the LARS, ROV, and ROC cybersecurity architecture; and (3) the operational logistics model — whether the USV operates from a fixed shore base, a support vessel, or a hybrid of both — which determines transit endurance requirements and the degree of autonomous self-sufficiency required between deployments.
